title: Aging Our Way: Lessons for Living from 85 and Beyond By Meika Loe Oxford University Press. 2011. 344 pages. $29.95 cloth
abstract: Aging Our Way moves the discussion of aging, as Loe states, past the ever popular antiaging philosophies and theories of active, successful aging. Such theories are thought to focus the experience of aging along one path, favoring multiple dimensions of optimal health with high levels of social engagement. Without a study of meaningful aging as assessed on elders' own terms, we would not authentically learn more about lived experiences of the “oldest old”—a term for those aged 85 years of age and older and the focus of Loe's book. Studying the oldest old has long been a focus on gerontology and sociology, with Suzman and Riley's “Introduction to the ‘Oldest Old’” (1985), followed by Suzman, Willis and Manton's text in 1995. As with these original studies, Loe's book arrives at a time when those aged 85 years and older are undergoing great change, but differs from these previous works in its more qualitative and person-centered focus, incorporating current threads in the discourse on aging, for example, the paradigmatic shift towards a model of meaningful aging and work focusing solely on those aged 85 years and older (e.g., the first U.S. Census Report, in 2011, “90+ in the United States”). Loe's book addresses the call of contemporary peers, such as Rowles and Manning in Experiencing the Third Age: The Practice of Qualitative Inquiry (2011), and uses a participatory action approach, one in which the elder, environment and researcher all interact in unison. Loe addresses key questions raised in Poon and Cohen-Mansfield's Understanding Well-Being in the Oldest Old (2011), namely, to understand factors uniquely related to well-being for this group.
